result: Morning light slips through the blinds and paints thin stripes across the desk, and I feel the urge to write anything, everything, the way a river keeps moving even when it doesn't know the shape of its mouth. There is coffee cooling beside me, a faint smell of rain from an open window, and a scattered pile of notes that resemble a tiny storm of paper. The city outside is waking too, with buses coughing down the street, with a bicyclist humming, with someone laughing into a phone. I imagine the threads of all these sounds weaving together, forming a fabric the day will wear. My mind wanders to old walks, the ones where I tried to map my neighborhood by smell instead of sight. Bread from the bakery, gasoline near the station, jasmine climbing a fence, the sharp tang of autumn leaves underfoot. Memory is a scent, and some afternoons still drift into me as if the door were left open. I think of the time I read about monks raking patterns into sand, then smoothing them away. The effort seemed both pointless and luminous, like breathing on a mirror. Perhaps writing is similar, a temporary arrangement, a way to acknowledge that shapes exist even if they vanish. Words are footprints in weather. A bird hops on the sill, head cocked, as though it might listen. I whisper a greeting and it answers by flicking its tail. There is a whole world beyond the window, of course, but I am learning how wide a small room can be. The desk surface is a continent of pencils, old stamps, a shell picked up years ago on a coast that might have been the edge of the world. Sometimes I hold that shell to my ear, and it is not the ocean I hear but the rush of my own blood, the body’s tide. I wonder how much of our experience is internal, how many landscapes are hidden behind the ribs. The afternoon might bring errands, the evening might bring a friend, and somewhere between those moments there will be blank stretches where time feels like blank paper. I like those stretches. They give a chance to hear the clock, to notice the lengthening shadows, to remember that a life can be both ordinary and miraculous. My grandmother once told me that a miracle was a small thing, like a seed splitting or an apology offered. She kept a jar of buttons, and would let me sift them, each one cold and colored like a tiny planet. I have inherited that jar, and sometimes I pour the buttons onto the table and arrange them into constellations. It is a kind of play and a kind of prayer. The world feels restless lately, its news scrolling like a storm, but in this room I can choose slowness. I can choose to listen to the way the radiator ticks, to watch the dust spin in sunlight, to trace the crack in the ceiling as if it were a river on a map. I have never been good at knowing where I am going, but I am learning to appreciate where I am. There is a notebook with pages still empty, and that emptiness is not a threat but an invitation. I think about the child I used to be, the one who thought the moon followed the car because it liked us. That child still sits in my chest, bright eyed, convinced the universe is personal. Maybe it is. Maybe the universe answers in coincidence, in the way a song on the radio matches your mood, in the way you meet a stranger and feel you already know them. I have met many strangers, some like passing rain, others like windows thrown open. At a train station once, a woman with red hair told me about a town she loved, a place with windmills and a blue river. I never went there, but it exists in my mind, spinning quietly whenever I need a landscape of hope. The clock shows that the morning is nearly gone, and still I am writing, the words arriving like small birds, perching briefly on the line before flying off. I think of letters written but never sent, of poems hidden in drawers, of conversations that happened only in the mind. All of it is part of the vast unfinished draft that is a life. If I could speak to the future, I would not ask for certainty. I would ask for curiosity, for the ability to keep looking, to keep listening, to keep being surprised. I would ask for the courage to be kind even when the world is sharp. The sun has shifted, and the stripes on the desk have moved. The morning is now a different room. I take a sip of cool coffee, think about lunch, about the ache in my shoulders, about the long line at the market where people will stand with baskets of oranges and spinach. I imagine the cashier smiling, the way she sometimes does when she recognizes a face. We are all carrying our invisible histories, and when they brush against each other there is a brief spark. That spark is what keeps me attentive. That spark is why I keep writing, even on an ordinary morning, even with no plan. The page is nearly full, a harbor of ink. Soon I will close the notebook and step outside. The air will be warm, the pavement will smell of sun, and the day will keep unfolding like a map I do not yet know how to read. I will read it anyway, with my whole body, with my imperfect sense of direction, with my hope that each turn holds a story. For now the room is quiet except for the pen, and the pen is a small oar in the current of thought. I row a little farther, then let the river carry me, content to be afloat today.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
